cue: How do you say two people are identical?

es.text: Parecerse como dos gotas de agua
es.literal: to resemble each other like two drops of water
es.example: Los gemelos se parecen como dos gotas de agua.
es.exampleTranslation: The twins are like two peas in a pod.

it.literal: to resemble each other like two drops of water
it.example: I gemelli si somigliano come due gocce d'acqua.
it.exampleTranslation: The twins are like two peas in a pod.

bridge.note: A near-perfect twin about twins — the two languages use the very same image, two drops of water, to mean identical. English is the odd one out, reaching for peas in a pod. The only gap is grammatical, and it is the same reflexive both languages build differently: Spanish parecerSE, Italian somigliarSI, each with its own pronoun. Same picture, same meaning, two ways of saying 'each other'.

interference.es_to_it: The verb is somigliarsi (or assomigliarsi), not parersi — and the drops are gocce, plural of goccia.
interference.it_to_es: Spanish uses parecerse, not an equivalent of somigliare; 'dos gotas de agua' is exactly right.

review.notes: ES side discovered in the Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh). IT counterpart from Claude's recall — UNVERIFIED. Freebie twin; the reflexive verbs are the small lesson. English's peas make a nice footnote.
